channel.title: Cron on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Cron on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Laravel ScheduleTaskFailedException Fix
item.description:
Laravel ScheduleTaskFailedException

Fix scheduled task failures in Laravel. Handle cron configuration, task errors, and notification failures..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access denied

Common Causes

<?php
// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing setup
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources
?>

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and setup

<?php
// Check configuration values and ensure required setup
// Verify the package/library is properly configured
?>

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

<?php
try {
 // Use the package/library with proper error handling
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 // Handle gracefully
}
?>

Fix 3: Validate input and add checks

<?php
// Validate input before processing
// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons
?>

Examples

<?php
// Common error handling pattern
try {
 $result = doSomething();
 echo $result;
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 http_response_code(500);
 echo json_encode(['error' => $e->getMessage()]);
}
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Fatal Error — fatal error
  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] Linux CRON Error — CMD failed / Execution Error Fix
item.description:
Linux: CRON - (root) CMD failed / error

The CRON: (root) CMD failed error means a scheduled cron job failed to execute. Cron silently runs jobs and emails output (if configured), but when the command itself fails or the environment isn’t set up correctly, errors appear in system logs. Cron errors are notoriously difficult to debug because the execution environment differs from an interactive shell.

Common Causes

  • Command or script path is incorrect or missing
  • Script lacks execute permissions
  • Cron environment lacks PATH and other variables
  • Output redirection errors
  • Script depends on services or resources not available at cron time
  • Crontab syntax errors (wrong field format)

How to Fix

1. Check Cron Logs

# Check cron logs (Debian/Ubuntu)
grep CRON /var/log/syslog

# Check cron logs (RHEL/CentOS/Fedora)
journalctl -u crond
cat /var/log/cron

# Check for errors in the last hour
grep -i "error\|failed\|CMD" /var/log/syslog | grep CRON | tail -20

2. Fix Crontab Syntax

# Edit the crontab
crontab -e

# View the current crontab
crontab -l

# Verify crontab syntax (install cron-check if available)
# Or use crontab.guru for validation: https://crontab.guru

Correct cron format:

item.title: [Solution] Linux crontab Installation Error — Fix
item.description:
Linux: crontab: installation error

The crontab: installation error message means the crontab command failed to save or install a new crontab file. This typically happens when the crontab directory is full, permissions are wrong, or the cron daemon has issues reading the spool directory.

What This Error Means

When you run crontab -e, the edited crontab is saved to /var/spool/cron/crontabs/ (Debian/Ubuntu) or /var/spool/cron/ (RHEL/CentOS). The cron daemon (cron or crond) watches this directory and loads new crontabs on changes. An “installation error” means the temporary file couldn’t be moved to the spool directory, or cron couldn’t read it.

item.title: [Solution] node-cron Scheduling Error Fix
item.description:
node-cron Scheduling Error

Fix node-cron scheduling errors when cron jobs fail to execute..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access issues

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing dependencies
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and dependencies

// Check configuration values and ensure required dependencies are installed
// Verify the module/package is properly configured for your environment

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

try {
 // Use the module/package with proper error handling
} catch (err) {
 console.error('Error:', err.message);
 // Handle gracefully
}

Fix 3: Add retry and timeout logic

// For network operations, add timeout and retry logic
// For file operations, check existence before accessing

Examples

// Common error handling pattern
try {
 // Operation that may fail
} catch (err) {
 if (err.code === 'ECONNREFUSED') {
 console.error('Connection refused - check if service is running');
 } else if (err.code === 'ETIMEDOUT') {
 console.error('Operation timed out');
 }
}

Related Errors

  • ECONNREFUSED — connection refused
  • ETIMEDOUT — timeout
  • ENOENT — file not found

item.title: [Solution] node-cron Syntax Error Fix
item.description:
node-cron Syntax Error

Fix node-cron syntax errors. Handle invalid cron expressions and scheduling configuration..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access issues

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing dependencies
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources

// Check configuration values and ensure required dependencies are installed
// Verify the module/package is properly configured for your environment

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

try {
 // Use the module/package with proper error handling
} catch (err) {
 console.error('Error:', err.message);
 // Handle gracefully
}

Fix 3: Add retry and timeout logic

// For network operations, add timeout and retry logic
// For file operations, check existence before accessing

Examples

// Common error handling pattern
try {
 // Operation that may fail
} catch (err) {
 if (err.code === 'ECONNREFUSED') {
 console.error('Connection refused - check if service is running');
 } else if (err.code === 'ETIMEDOUT') {
 console.error('Operation timed out');
 }
}

Related Errors

  • ECONNREFUSED — connection refused
  • ETIMEDOUT — timeout
  • ENOENT — file not found

item.title: [Solution] node-schedule Job Error Fix
item.description:
node-schedule Job Error

Fix node-schedule job errors. Handle scheduling failures, timezone issues, and job execution problems..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access issues

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing dependencies
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and dependencies

// Check configuration values and ensure required dependencies are installed
// Verify the module/package is properly configured for your environment

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

try {
 // Use the module/package with proper error handling
} catch (err) {
 console.error('Error:', err.message);
 // Handle gracefully
}

Fix 3: Add retry and timeout logic

// For network operations, add timeout and retry logic
// For file operations, check existence before accessing

Examples

// Common error handling pattern
try {
 // Operation that may fail
} catch (err) {
 if (err.code === 'ECONNREFUSED') {
 console.error('Connection refused - check if service is running');
 } else if (err.code === 'ETIMEDOUT') {
 console.error('Operation timed out');
 }
}

Related Errors

  • ECONNREFUSED — connection refused
  • ETIMEDOUT — timeout
  • ENOENT — file not found

item.title: ScheduleTaskFailedException
item.description:
This error occurs when a Laravel scheduled task throws an exception during execution. The scheduler catches the exception and reports it via the configured failure reporting channel.

Common Causes

  • Scheduled command throws an uncaught exception
  • Database connection lost during task execution
  • Task depends on a service that is unavailable
  • File or resource not accessible from the scheduler context
  • Permission issues when running as cron job

How to Fix

  1. Wrap scheduled task logic in try-catch:
class SendWeeklyReport extends Command
{
 protected $signature = 'report:weekly';

 public function handle(): int
 {
 try {
 $users = User::where('active', true)->get();

 foreach ($users as $user) {
 Mail::to($user)->send(new WeeklyReport($user));
 }

 return self::SUCCESS;
 } catch (\Exception $e) {
 $this->error('Report failed: ' . $e->getMessage());
 Log::error('Weekly report failed', ['exception' => $e]);
 return self::FAILURE;
 }
 }
}
  1. Configure failure notifications in Console/Kernel.php:
protected function schedule(Schedule $schedule): void
{
 $schedule->command('report:weekly')
 ->daily()
 ->appendOutputTo(storage_path('logs/scheduler.log'));
}
  1. Use onFailure callback in the schedule:
$schedule->command('report:weekly')
 ->daily()
 ->onFailure(function () {
 Log::error('Scheduled task failed');
 });

Examples

// Kernel.php
$schedule->command('orders:process')->everyFiveMinutes();
// ScheduleTaskFailedException if database is unreachable

Related Errors

  • Queue error
  • Horizon error

item.title: systemd Timer Error
item.description:
systemd Timer Error

A systemd timer error occurs when a timer unit fails to trigger its associated service on schedule. Timers are systemd’s replacement for cron jobs.

Common Causes

  • Timer unit file syntax errors
  • Service unit referenced by timer does not exist
  • Timer calendar expression is invalid
  • System time or timezone issues

How to Fix

Check Timer Status

systemctl status mytimer.timer
systemctl list-timers --all

Verify Timer Configuration

# /etc/systemd/system/mytimer.timer
[Unit]
Description=My Timer

[Timer]
OnCalendar=daily
Persistent=true

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target

Check Service Unit Exists

systemctl cat mytimer.service
# Ensure the service unit exists

Validate Calendar Expression

systemd-analyze calendar "daily"
systemd-analyze calendar "Mon *-*-* 09:00:00"
systemd-analyze calendar --iterations=5 "hourly"

Enable and Start Timer

sudo systemctl enable mytimer.timer
sudo systemctl start mytimer.timer

Check Timer Logs

journalctl -u mytimer.service -n 20

Examples

systemctl status backup.timer
backup.timer: Failed to start.

# Fix: check timer configuration
systemd-analyze verify backup.timer

Related Errors

  • Unit Start Failed — service start failure
  • Service Not Found — service does not exist
